choices: A family of signals (unusual client, unusual location, token theft, MFA-fatigue, credential-stuffing) rather than a single pattern.

choices.label: A lower-severity version of impossible travel.
choices.rationale: Different category, different signals, different patterns.

choices.label: An EDR signal, not an ITDR one.
choices.rationale: Suspicious sign-in is identity-side.

choices.label: A signal that only fires on admin accounts.
choices.rationale: Fires on any account.

questions.explanation: A family, not a single pattern. The Recommendation tells you the specific shape of this one.

questions.prompt: Suspicious sign-in often co-occurs with another category. How does the SOC route it?

choices.label: Suspicious sign-in is always the primary category when both apply.
choices.rationale: Inverted. The more specific category wins.

choices: The SOC routes to the most specific category that fits. When the primary is 'suspicious sign-in,' the sign-in itself is the signal.

choices.label: The SOC raises two incidents, one for each category.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the platform behaviour.

choices.label: The tech routes the incident manually after triage.
choices.rationale: The SOC does the categorisation.

questions.explanation: The more specific category (inbox rule, OAuth, MFA tampering) takes primary when it applies.

questions.prompt: "If no follow-on activity, no compromise." Why is this wrong?

choices.label: Follow-on activity is the only signal the SOC uses.
choices.rationale: Sign-in signals alone can drive disposition.

choices.label: All sign-ins always have follow-on activity.
choices.rationale: Some sign-ins genuinely have no follow-on yet.

choices: Attackers establish access and wait. The SOC's classification weighs the sign-in's own signals. A high-confidence-suspicious sign-in with no follow-on is still a compromise to address.

choices.label: Lack of follow-on confirms the compromise rather than ruling it out.
choices.rationale: Overstates in the opposite direction.

questions.explanation: No-follow-on is not the disposition. The SOC's classification has already considered it.

questions.prompt: "User accepting the MFA prompt means it was them." Why is this wrong on MFA-fatigue attacks?

choices.label: MFA-fatigue is rare enough that acceptance counts as confirmation.
choices.rationale: Wrong on prevalence and policy.

choices.label: Users never accept MFA prompts.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the facts.

choices.label: MFA prompts cannot be accepted by accident.
choices.rationale: The whole point of fatigue attacks is accidental acceptance.

choices: Under MFA fatigue, the user accepts to make spammed prompts stop, not because they intended to sign in. The Timeline's prompt timing reveals the pattern.
questions.explanation: Accepted-under-duress is not user intent. Seven push prompts in two minutes at 3am is the fatigue pattern.

questions.prompt: High-severity: seven MFA prompts between 03:11-03:13, seventh accepted. Residential-proxy IP, unfamiliar country. No follow-on. Recommendation: revoke sessions, reset password and MFA, contact user. What is the right interpretation?

choices.label: Tomas approved the MFA, so the sign-in was probably him. Close as benign.
choices.rationale: MFA-fatigue is a known attack pattern. The Recommendation classified this as compromise.

choices: Execute the playbook (revoke sessions, reset password and MFA), then call Tomas to explain the fatigue pattern and confirm he was not intending to sign in at 03:13.

choices.label: Call Tomas first to ask whether he was signing in at 03:13.
choices.rationale: Same blast-radius reasoning. Even with no follow-on, the SOC's classification is compromise.

questions.explanation: Execute first. The user education during the post-action call prevents the same mistake next time.

questions.prompt: Tomas confirms: 'I was getting prompts at 3am, I just tapped approve to make them stop.' What is the right framing?

choices.label: Apologise for waking him.
choices.rationale: The MSP does not apologise for response calls on actual compromise.

choices.label: Tell him tersely: 'Do not approve random MFA prompts.'
choices.rationale: Too terse. Tomas needs the why and the protective framing to build the habit.

choices: Explain that the prompts were an attacker, that tapping approve let them sign in, that containment is complete, and that he should deny unsolicited prompts and call the helpdesk instead.
questions.explanation: Educate on the pattern (prevents repeat), name what you did, give clear next steps.

questions.prompt: "Suspicious sign-in is just a lower-severity version of impossible travel." What is the cleanest correction?

choices.label: Suspicious sign-in is always Critical, impossible-travel is always High.
choices.rationale: Wrong on severity bands.

choices.label: Correct; it is a lower-severity version.
choices.rationale: They are different categories.

choices: Different signals, different category. Impossible-travel is about geographic impossibility. Suspicious-sign-in is the broader family of 'something is off about this sign-in.'

choices.label: Impossible-travel is an EDR signal, suspicious sign-in is ITDR.
choices.rationale: Both are ITDR.

questions.explanation: Categories are distinct, not severity tiers of the same thing.

questions.prompt: Why is user verification more common on suspicious sign-in than on the other ITDR incident types?

choices.label: Verification is not more common; the lesson is wrong.
choices.rationale: The lesson names this explicitly.

choices.label: Verification is required by Huntress policy on this category specifically.
choices.rationale: Not a documented platform requirement.

choices.label: The user always knows more than the SOC on this category.
choices.rationale: Overstates. The SOC has cross-customer pattern visibility.

choices: The SOC sometimes needs the user's input to confirm whether the activity was theirs. Suspicious-sign-in classifications include cases that are benign-but-flagged-for-visibility.
questions.explanation: The category covers ambiguous-by-design cases where user input helps the SOC's classification.

questions.prompt: The Recommendation says 'verify with user, then proceed with the playbook if not confirmed.' What does that mean in practice?

choices.label: Skip the playbook unless the user actively asks for it.
choices.rationale: Default is to proceed if not confirmed.

choices: The SOC has classified the activity as likely-suspicious but wants a user check before the destructive playbook runs. If the user denies or is unreachable on active-exfiltration patterns, proceed.

choices.label: Wait indefinitely until the user is reachable.
choices.rationale: Lesson 8 handles the unreachable case. Cost-of-waiting still applies.

choices.label: Run the playbook in parallel with the verification call.
choices.rationale: The Recommendation explicitly puts verification first.

questions.explanation: Verification is the gating step the SOC has chosen. If the user denies or is unreachable, the playbook proceeds.

questions.prompt: What does a credential-stuffing pattern look like in the sign-in events?

choices.label: A single successful sign-in from the user's normal location.
choices.rationale: No anomaly. Would not fire suspicious-sign-in.

choices.label: A sign-in from two countries in five minutes.
choices.rationale: That is the impossible-travel shape, not credential-stuffing.

choices: Multiple failed sign-ins from one source followed by a success, suggesting password-spray or credential-list attacks.

choices.label: A sign-in with no MFA challenge.
choices.rationale: Missing MFA challenge alone is not the credential-stuffing pattern.

questions.explanation: Failed-then-success from a single source is the credential-stuffing pattern.
